nameMeanings.name: de
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Dilip, Anirban, Gopal, Alok, Anindya, Arunava, Asit, Asok, Dibyendu, Goutam, Indra, Indranil.

variants.name: Dey
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Ashit, Indu, Naina, Partha, Soumya, Ajit, Anjan, Anjana, Anup, Arjun, Arun, Ashim.
English, Scottish, and Welsh: variant of   Day  .
German: topographic name from a field name in North Rhine-Westphalia, denoting a sizeable piece of land, from Middle Low German dē ‘thigh’ (referring to the shape of the property).

nameMeanings.name: Pechey
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of Family Names in Britain and Ireland © University of the West of England 2016

variants.name: Peachey
meanings: Americanized form of Swiss German Bütschi (see Butsch ), via the intermediate form Bitsche. This surname is most common in PA. Compare Beachy , Beechy , and Peachy .

History: This (see 1 above) is the name of a Swiss Mennonite (Amish) family, in the US also spelled Beachy, Beachey, Beechy, and Peachy. The family is most numerous in PA and in parts of OH and IN. Its progenitor was Peter Bitsche who came to North America from Switzerland in 1767 and settled in PA. The original Swiss German form of his surname was Bütschi (it originates from Bern canton where it is still most frequent), whereas Bitsche is otherwise an Austrian surname, apparently taken to Switzerland only in recent times.

variants.name: Petchey
meanings: Norman: nickname from Old French pecch(i)et, pech(i)é (Latin peccatum) ‘fault, sin’. The name has varied freely between the Old French form (with final -(i)é spelled -(i)e, -ee, and -y) and the synonymous Middle English form pec(c)he ( see Petch ). The stressed vowel in Pecché was short, so Petchey and Petch are closest to the original pronunciation of the name, but Peachey and Peach, with a lengthened vowel, are now much commoner.

nameMeanings.name: Simon
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Usual English form of Simeon , borne in the New Testament by various characters: two apostles, a brother of Jesus, a Pharisee, a leper, a tanner, a sorcerer (who offered money for the gifts of the Holy Ghost, giving rise to the term simony), and the man who carried Jesus's cross to the Crucifixion.

variants.name: Simen
meanings: Usual English form of Simeon , borne in the New Testament by various characters: two apostles, a brother of Jesus, a Pharisee, a leper, a tanner, a sorcerer (who offered money for the gifts of the Holy Ghost, giving rise to the term simony), and the man who carried Jesus's cross to the Crucifixion.

historicEvents.title: First War of Scottish Independence
historicEvents.description: The First War of Scottish Independence began after England invaded Scotland in 1296. The Treaty of Edinburgh was signed in 1328 which declared peace between the two kingdoms and brought the war to an end.

historicEvents.title: The Hundred Years' War
historicEvents.description: The Hundred Years' War between France and England was largely due to the question of legitimate succession to the French crown.

historicEvents.title: Wycliffe's Bible
historicEvents.description: John Wycliffe, English theologian and professor at the University of Oxford, completed a translation of the Bible from the Latin Vulgate into Middle English. This translation is known as Wycliffe's Bible and was completed around 1382.

en.lifeSummary: When Simon de Pechey was born about 1322, in Suffolk, England, his father, Gilbert III Pecche 1st Lord Peche, was 55 and his mother, Iseult, was 43. He had at least 1 son and 1 daughter with Agnes Holme. He died in 1398, at the age of 77, and was buried in England, United Kingdom.
